title: Matrix Operations
description: Arithmetic on matrices, transpose (t()), and matrix multiplication (%*%).

slides.title: Element-wise Arithmetic ➕
slides.content:
Just like vectors, matrices support element-wise arithmetic when you use `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `^`. They must have the same dimensions, or one can be a scalar (recycled).

```r
m1 <- matrix(1:4, nrow=2)
m2 <- matrix(5:8, nrow=2)
m1 + m2   # element-wise addition
m1 * 10   # multiply every element by 10
```

slides.title: Element-wise Ops
slides.codeSnippet:
m1 <- matrix(1:4, nrow=2)
m2 <- matrix(5:8, nrow=2)
print(m1 + m2)
print(m1 * m2)
print(m1 ^ 2)

quizData.question: What is the result of `matrix(1:4, nrow=2) * matrix(2:5, nrow=2)`?
quizData.explanation: `*` does element-wise multiplication, not matrix multiplication.

slides.title: Transpose with t() 🔄
slides.content:
The transpose of a matrix swaps rows and columns. Use `t()`.

```r
m <- matrix(1:6, nrow=2)
t(m)   # becomes 3x2
```

slides.title: Matrix Multiplication %*%
slides.content:
For true matrix multiplication (dot product), use `%*%`. The number of columns of the first must equal the number of rows of the second.

```r
A <- matrix(1:4, nrow=2)
B <- matrix(5:8, nrow=2)
A %*% B   # 2x2 matrix
```

This is not element-wise.

slides.title: Matrix Multiplication
slides.codeSnippet:
A <- matrix(1:4, nrow=2, byrow=TRUE)
B <- matrix(5:8, nrow=2)
print(A)
print(B)
print(A %*% B)

quizData.question: Given A is 2x3 and B is 3x4, what is the dimension of A %*% B?
quizData.explanation: Matrix multiplication results in a matrix with rows from first and columns from second: 2x4.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: For element-wise multiplication, use `*`. For matrix multiplication, use `%*%`. Don't confuse them!
